title: Find the hidden FX margin in 30 seconds

scripts.subtitle: Classic PAS opener
scripts.original_vo: Listen. Open your last wire confirmation right now. If you're a business owner paying suppliers in China, you're about to find a fee your bank never showed you. Three numbers. That's all you need. Number one. The applied rate. It's printed at the bottom of your confirmation. Find it. Number two. The interbank rate. Google USD to CNY for the exact time the wire was booked. Number three. Your wire amount. Now subtract applied from interbank. Divide by interbank. Multiply by 100. That percent is the foreign exchange margin your bank quietly took. Example. Applied 7.05. Interbank 7.22. The gap is 2.35 percent. On a 50,000 dollar wire, that's 1,175 dollars gone. Stop. WorldFirst shows you the rate upfront. One rate. No hidden margin. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

scripts.subtitle: 30-second timer + brutal specificity
scripts.original_vo: Thirty seconds. That's all it takes to find the foreign exchange margin your bank hid in your last wire to China. Watch. Step one. Pull up your wire confirmation. Five seconds. Find the field that says applied rate or exchange rate used. Got it. Step two. Open a new tab. Google USD to CNY. Look at the rate. Note the time. Ten seconds. Step three. The math. Interbank minus applied. Divide by interbank. Times 100. Ten seconds. That's the foreign exchange margin percent. Five seconds to multiply by your wire amount. That's the dollar amount they took. Real example. Applied 7.05. Interbank that morning 7.22. Margin 2.35 percent. On 50,000 dollars, you paid 1,175 dollars in invisible foreign exchange spread. The wire fee on the receipt? Forty bucks. The real cost? Thirty times that. Stop. WorldFirst shows you the rate before you click send. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

scripts.subtitle: Accusation opener + receipt walkthrough
scripts.original_vo: Your bank charged you 1,175 dollars on your last wire to China and didn't put it on the receipt. Here's how to find it in thirty seconds. Open your wire confirmation. Look near the bottom. There's a line called applied rate or exchange rate used. Write that number down. 7.05. That's what your bank used to convert USD to CNY. Now open Google. Type USD to CNY. Look at the live rate. 7.22. Same day, same hour. That's the interbank rate, the rate banks trade with each other. The gap between 7.22 and 7.05 is the foreign exchange margin your bank took. Math. 7.22 minus 7.05 equals 0.17. 0.17 divided by 7.22 times 100 equals 2.35 percent. On a 50,000 dollar wire, that's 1,175 dollars. Not on the receipt. Stop. WorldFirst shows the rate upfront. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Search WorldFirst.

scripts.subtitle: Mechanism-first tutorial
scripts.original_vo: Here's something most business owners don't know. The biggest cost on your wire to China isn't on your wire confirmation. It's hidden inside the exchange rate they used. Foreign exchange margin. Here's how to find it in thirty seconds. You need three numbers. One. The applied rate. The exchange rate printed on your confirmation. Usually 7-point-something for USD to CNY. Two. The interbank rate. Google USD to CNY at the same date and time the wire was booked. This is the real rate banks pay each other. Three. Your wire amount in USD. Now subtract applied from interbank. Divide that by interbank. Multiply by 100. That percent is what your bank quietly added to the rate. Multiply that percent by your wire amount. That's the dollar cost. Most SEA bank confirmations show the applied rate but never the interbank reference. By design. Stop. WorldFirst shows you both. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Multiply that percent by your wire amount. That's the dollar cost. Most SEA bank confirmations show the applied rate but never the interbank reference. By design. WorldFirst shows you both. Search WorldFirst.

scripts.subtitle: Direct address + scaled math
scripts.original_vo: If you wire money to China, you need to do this once. Today. Right now. Pull up your last wire confirmation. Find the applied rate. The exchange rate your bank used to convert your USD to CNY. Write it down. 7.05. Open Google. Type USD to CNY. Note the rate at that moment. 7.22. That's the foreign exchange interbank rate. The real wholesale price. The gap between those two numbers, expressed as a percent, is what your bank charged you on top of the wire fee. 7.22 minus 7.05, divided by 7.22, times 100. 2.35 percent. On 50,000 dollars, that's 1,175 dollars. Do six wires a year? Seven thousand dollars. Twelve wires? Fourteen thousand. None of it on the receipt. Stop signing off on this. WorldFirst shows you the rate before you click send. One rate. No surprise margin. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

scripts.subtitle: Real-talk opener + thirty-second challenge
scripts.original_vo: Stop. Real talk for every business owner sending wires to China. You don't know what your bank actually charged you. You think you do. You don't. The wire fee on the receipt? That's the smallest part of the cost. The real cost is the foreign exchange margin baked into the rate. And it takes thirty seconds to find it. Open your last wire confirmation. Find the applied rate. Google the interbank rate at the same moment. Subtract. Divide. Times 100. That percent is the silent fee. Most of you are paying 1.5 to 3 percent on top of the wire fee and have no idea. That's hundreds, sometimes thousands per payment. WorldFirst shows you the rate upfront. Worth checking. Search WorldFirst. Now.

scripts.subtitle: I'll say it + the math nobody runs
scripts.original_vo: I'll say it. Most business owners I talk to have never once checked the foreign exchange margin on their wire to China. Not once. They look at the wire fee. Forty bucks. They feel good. They click send. They don't run the math that takes thirty seconds. Applied rate on the confirmation. Interbank rate from Google at the same time. Subtract. Divide. Multiply by 100. Two percent. Two and a half. Sometimes three. On a 50,000 dollar wire, that's 1,000 to 1,500 dollars gone. Per wire. Multiply that across a year of payments. You're hemorrhaging cash and the receipt says forty dollars. That's the trick. The fee is on the rate. Not the line item. WorldFirst puts the rate on the screen before you send. One number. No surprise. Search WorldFirst.

scripts.subtitle: Stop trusting the receipt + reframe
scripts.original_vo: Stop trusting your wire receipt. I'm serious. The line that says wire fee, forty dollars, that's not your real cost. The real cost is the foreign exchange margin and you can find it in thirty seconds. Look. Your confirmation shows an applied rate. That's the rate your bank used to convert. Google shows the interbank rate. That's the wholesale rate. The gap between those two numbers is what your bank quietly took on top. On USD-CNY, that gap is usually 1.5 to 3 percent. On a 50,000 dollar wire, that's up to 1,500 dollars. The receipt will never tell you this. The receipt is designed to make you feel good about forty dollars. So stop looking at the receipt. Look at the rate. WorldFirst puts the rate up front. The number you see is the number you get. Search WorldFirst. Now.

scripts.subtitle: Truth nobody's telling you
scripts.original_vo: Here's the truth nobody's telling you about wire transfers to China. The fee on the receipt is bait. It's there to anchor your attention. Forty bucks. Feels cheap. So you don't look further. But the real money is on the rate. The applied rate on your confirmation versus the interbank rate at the same time. That gap is the foreign exchange margin. It's never broken out. Never disclosed. By design. Banks know if they put 'plus 2.35 percent foreign exchange margin' on the receipt, you'd shop around. So they hide it inside the rate. Thirty seconds of math reveals it. Pull the applied rate. Pull the interbank rate. Run the formula. There's the number you actually paid. Could be 1,000 dollars per wire. Could be more. Every time. WorldFirst shows the rate before you click send. One number. Period.

PH1.analysis: Core tutorial is mechanically accurate: applied rate is genuinely printed on most SEA bank wire confirmations, the interbank reference can be checked via Google/XE, and the formula ((Interbank - Applied) / Interbank) * 100 yields the margin percent. The 7.05 / 7.22 / 2.35% / $1,175 worked example is internally consistent and reflects realistic spreads. 'WorldFirst shows you the rate upfront' is broadly true via the live-rate display in the platform. Main soft spot: interbank rate varies minute-to-minute, so the user's number will rarely match the example exactly.

skeptic_comments.text: Where got applied rate on Maybank confirmation? Mine only show CNY amount.
Google rate not same as the rate my bank can actually get. Wholesale and retail different lah.
Who is WorldFirst? Got Bank Negara license or not?
2.35% sounds high. My HSBC SME rate is much better than that.
Thirty seconds my foot. By the time I find the confirmation in email already five minutes.

PH2.analysis: Strong tutorial structure with verifiable numbers. The thirty-second framing is aspirational but the steps themselves are accurate. The $40 wire fee comparison is realistic for SEA SME wires. 'Thirty times that' is a clean math claim from 1,175 / 40 = 29.4x, which checks out for the specific example. The 'by design' framing about banks not showing interbank reference is supported by the findings array. Minor risk: assumes the user can find the applied rate field, which varies in label across banks.

skeptic_comments.text: My wire fee is 80 RM not 40 dollars. Different bank different fee leh.
Applied rate not always in obvious spot. CIMB hides it inside PDF attachment.
Thirty times the fee? Sounds too dramatic. You sure not exaggerate?
Got Malaysian-licensed alternative or just Singapore?
Interbank rate moves every second. Which exact moment to compare?

PH3.analysis: Mechanism is accurate but the accusation framing ('pocketed') is strong. Banks legally disclose the applied rate, so calling it hidden is rhetorically true but legally fragile - the spread is a margin, not theft, and is standard industry practice. The math is correct: 0.17 / 7.22 * 100 = 2.354%. Example assumes user wires exactly $50K, which the tutorial then anchors on. 'Not on the receipt' is mostly true but some banks do disclose margin separately in fee schedules, just not on the confirmation. Trust risk if a skeptic checks their own confirmation and finds different formatting.

skeptic_comments.text: Pocketed sounds like accusation. My bank not stealing, just charging margin.
$1,175 on $50K is 2.35%, but my last wire was way smaller. Math don't scale to my situation.
Receipt do show applied rate la. Just need to read carefully.
Search WorldFirst? In Malaysia I search and got mixed results. Confusing.
Why use 7.22 not the rate at booking time? Google rate is delayed.

PH4.analysis: Most educationally accurate version. Defines foreign exchange margin clearly on first mention. Three-numbers structure is faithful to the source findings. 'Most SEA bank confirmations show the applied rate but never the interbank reference' is a direct quote from the findings array and accurate. 'WorldFirst shows you both' is the strongest verifiable product claim because the platform does display the live mid-rate at quote time. Lowest risk version of the pack.

skeptic_comments.text: By design what design? Bank legally required to show rate so what's hidden?
USD to CNY is 7-point-something but my bank quotes RM-CNY direct. Different scenario.
WorldFirst show both rates? Need to see screenshot before believe.
I'm in Malaysia not just SEA. Where's the MY-specific info?
How do I know Google rate is the real interbank? Could also be marked up.

PH5.analysis: Tutorial body is accurate. Risk concentration is in the annualised math: '6 wires = 7K, 12 wires = 14K' assumes consistent 2.35% margin and consistent $50K wire size, which won't hold for every viewer. The extrapolation is a fair illustration but presented as if universal. The applied 7.05 / interbank 7.22 specific example is fine. 'None of it on the receipt' overreaches slightly because some banks do show partial breakdown in their FX confirmation document, just not the wire confirmation itself.

skeptic_comments.text: 6 wires a year? Some of us send 30+. Math feels light.
Or some of us only do 2 wires a year. So 2,300 not 14,000. Why scare us with big numbers?
WorldFirst no surprise margin? Sure or not? All FX have spread.
My SCB statement does show FX cost separately. Maybe not all banks hide.
What if my wire was at off-hours when interbank was different from Google now?

PG1.analysis: Solid opinion-led delivery. '1.5 to 3 percent' is an honest typical range for retail SME FX margins in SEA. 'Worth checking' is appropriately soft for an opinion piece. Risk: 'most of you are paying' is a population claim, not individual proof - some viewers will be on negotiated rates well below 1.5%. The brand-search CTA is brand-safe and doesn't overclaim. Mechanism (margin is in the rate, not the fee) is accurate.

skeptic_comments.text: Most of you? You speaking from data or just vibes?
My corporate rate is 0.5% not 2%. So this video not for me?
WorldFirst regulated where? I need to see license before sign up.
Subtract divide times 100 sounds easy on TikTok hard in real life.
Real talk back at you, where the screen recording proof?

PG2.analysis: Strong educational piece with clean math. 'Two percent. Two and a half. Sometimes three' is honest range-talk, not absolute claim. '$1,000 to $1,500' on $50K matches the math (2% to 3%). The pivot from receipt-fee anchoring to rate-margin reveal is structurally true. 'Per wire. Multiply across a year' is left as a thought-experiment rather than a fixed extrapolation, which is more honest. Brand CTA is restrained.

skeptic_comments.text: Forty dollars wire fee is US scenario. In Malaysia we pay differently.
$50K USD wire is huge. Most SME do $5K-$10K. Different math.
What's WorldFirst's actual rate? Show me before I trust the 'one number' claim.
Banks I talk to say their spread is much lower than 2%. Who is right?
Multiply across a year scary but I'm not gonna do 12 wires.

PG3.analysis: Receipt-as-bait framing is rhetorically aggressive but mechanically accurate. The applied vs interbank gap is verifiable. '1.5 to 3 percent' range is realistic for retail SME on USD-CNY. 'Up to 1,500 dollars' on 50K is correct at 3%. 'Receipt is designed to make you feel good about 40 dollars' is intent-attribution; some banks would dispute this is deliberate design vs regulatory convention. 'Number you see is the number you get' overpromises slightly because there's always a spread, even if disclosed upfront.

skeptic_comments.text: Designed to make me feel good? That's a conspiracy take. Receipt just follow standard format.
Number you see is number you get? Got hidden conversion at supplier end or not?
USD to CNY okay but my wires are SGD to CNY. Same problem?
Up to 1,500 dollars is range. Most of us are mid-range, not worst case.
WorldFirst Malaysia? Singapore? Hong Kong? Confusing which license applies.

PG4.analysis: 'Receipt is bait' is rhetorically charged but the underlying claim (the headline fee is small while the rate margin is bigger) is mechanically true. 'Never disclosed' is too absolute - some banks do disclose margin in fee schedules, just not at the wire level. 'By design' attributes intent to banks, which they would dispute. The 'plus 2.35 percent foreign exchange margin' hypothetical line is a strong rhetorical move but assumes regulator inaction; some markets (UK, EU) now require disclosure under PSD2-like rules. SEA is patchier. 'Could be more' is honest hedging.

skeptic_comments.text: Bait? You're calling all banks scammers? Tone too aggressive.
By design sounds like conspiracy theory. Show me the regulation that bans disclosure.
Never disclosed not true. My CIMB statement shows FX cost in monthly summary.
WorldFirst show before send is good but what about the actual rate they offer?
1,000 dollars per wire? Only if you wire 50K+. Many of us are way smaller.

PG5.analysis: Strongest interactive script. The pause-and-do-it-now framing puts the math in the viewer's hands so the claim becomes self-verifying rather than authority-claimed. '1.5 to 3 percent' range is honest and matches retail SEA reality. '$750 to $1,500 per wire on $50K' is internally consistent with the percent range. 'You did this yourself with thirty seconds and a calculator' is the strongest credibility move because it removes the trust-the-creator dependency. Brand mention is one line, restrained.

skeptic_comments.text: Pause and do it? Ya right. Most people just keep scrolling.
1.5 to 3 percent is wide range. Which one is my number?
My wire was 8K USD, not 50K. So 120 to 240 dollars. Not life-changing.

Need WorldFirst Malaysia presence info. Vague brand-search is not enough.
